---
title: "25 · n-Step Sarsa: Bridging TD(0) and Monte Carlo"
subtitle: "Bootstrap after n steps instead of 1: the bias-variance knob between TD and MC."
lesson-id: "mfrl.n-step-sarsa"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.sarsa"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you write the Sarsa update from memory? — see [MFRL — 24, Sarsa](../24-sarsa/index.qmd).
2. Can you state the Monte Carlo target — full discounted return $G_t$? —
   see [MFRL — 17, MC Basic](../17-mc-basic/index.qmd).
3. Comfortable with the bias/variance trade in TD vs MC? — recap in
   [MFRL — 23, TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd) Discover Problem 4.
4. Discount-factor identity $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \ldots$?

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

Pick $n$ — the number of steps before the bootstrap target kicks in.
$n = 1$ is plain Sarsa; $n = \infty$ is Monte Carlo. Anything in between is
**$n$-step Sarsa**.

The widget runs $n$-step Sarsa on a 10-state chain (start at left, terminal
+1 reward at right, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.2$). The blue line is the
running RMS error of $q$ versus $q^\*$ over 200 episodes. Sweep $n$ and
watch how the error curve changes.

```{ojs}
viewof nStep = Inputs.range([1, 30], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "n ="})
viewof nSeed = Inputs.range([1, 30], {value: 9, step: 1, label: "Seed:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  function mulberry32(a) {
    return function() {
      a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
      let t = a;
      t = Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 15, t | 1);
      t ^= t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, t | 61);
      return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
    };
  }
  const rand = mulberry32(nSeed * 19);
  const N = 10;            // states 0..9; goal at 9
  const A = 2;             // 0=left,1=right
  const gamma = 0.9, alpha = 0.2;
  const q = new Array(N * A).fill(0);
  const idx = (s, a) => s * A + a;
  function chooseAction(s) {
    if (rand() < 0.1) return Math.floor(rand() * A);
    return q[idx(s, 0)] >= q[idx(s, 1)] ? 0 : 1;
  }
  function step(s, a) {
    const ns = a === 0 ? Math.max(0, s - 1) : Math.min(N - 1, s + 1);
    const reward = ns === N - 1 ? 1 : 0;
    const done = ns === N - 1;
    return {ns, reward, done};
  }
  // Approximate q* by running for many episodes with n=1 first as truth.
  const qStar = new Array(N * A).fill(0);
  for (let s = 0; s < N - 1; s++) for (let a = 0; a < A; a++) {
    // Greedy expected return: from state s, going right repeatedly.
    const dist = (N - 1) - s;
    qStar[idx(s, a)] = a === 1 ? Math.pow(gamma, dist - 1) : Math.pow(gamma, dist + 1);
  }
  const errors = [];
  for (let ep = 0; ep < 200; ep++) {
    let s = 0, a = chooseAction(s);
    const traj = [{s, a, r: 0}];
    let T = Infinity;
    let t = 0;
    while (true) {
      if (t < T) {
        const {ns, reward, done} = step(traj[t].s, traj[t].a);
        const na = done ? 0 : chooseAction(ns);
        traj.push({s: ns, a: na, r: reward});
        if (done) T = t + 1;
      }
      const tau = t - nStep + 1;
      if (tau >= 0) {
        let G = 0;
        for (let i = tau + 1; i <= Math.min(tau + nStep, T); i++) {
          G += Math.pow(gamma, i - tau - 1) * traj[i].r;
        }
        if (tau + nStep < T) {
          G += Math.pow(gamma, nStep) * q[idx(traj[tau + nStep].s, traj[tau + nStep].a)];
        }
        const i0 = idx(traj[tau].s, traj[tau].a);
        q[i0] = q[i0] + alpha * (G - q[i0]);
      }
      if (tau === T - 1) break;
      t++;
      if (t > 200) break;
    }
    let err2 = 0, count = 0;
    for (let s = 0; s < N - 1; s++) for (let a = 0; a < A; a++) {
      const e = q[idx(s, a)] - qStar[idx(s, a)];
      err2 += e * e; count++;
    }
    errors.push({ep, rms: Math.sqrt(err2 / count)});
  }
  const Plot = await import("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@observablehq/plot@0.6/+esm");
  return Plot.plot({
    width: 540, height: 280,
    x: {label: "episode"},
    y: {label: "RMS error vs q*", domain: [0, 1.2]},
    marks: [
      Plot.line(errors, {x: "ep", y: "rms", stroke: "steelblue"})
    ]
  });
}
```

Try $n = 1$ (Sarsa), $n = 4$ (the Sutton-Barto sweet spot for this kind of
chain), $n = 10$ (close to MC). $n$ in the middle usually wins.

## Wonder

1. With $n = 1$, the reward at the goal must propagate back one state per
   episode. With $n = 5$, how far does the reward propagate per episode?
2. Larger $n$ means more real rewards in the target — and fewer guesses.
   Why might that *increase* variance?
3. The optimal $n$ depends on how noisy the environment is. Why?
4. If you average over *all* $n$ values weighted by $\lambda^{n-1}$, what
   famous algorithm do you get? (You don't need to know — just guess from the
   shape.)

## Discover

Six problems to derive the $n$-step return and connect TD to MC.

### Problem 1 — the $n$-step return

Recall $G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \ldots$. Truncate
at $n$ steps and bootstrap with $q$ at step $t + n$. Write the result.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$G_t^{(n)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n} + \gamma^n q(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n}).$$
This is the **$n$-step return**. Real rewards for $n$ steps, then bootstrap.
</details>

### Problem 2 — recover Sarsa and MC

What does $G_t^{(n)}$ become when $n = 1$? When $n \to \infty$ (assuming the
episode terminates)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$n = 1$: $G_t^{(1)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})$ — the **Sarsa
target**.
$n \to \infty$: $G_t^{(\infty)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \ldots = G_t$ — the
**Monte Carlo target**.
$n$-step Sarsa interpolates exactly between these two.
</details>

### Problem 3 — the $n$-step Sarsa update

Drop $G_t^{(n)}$ in as the target.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$q(s_t, a_t) \;\leftarrow\; q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \, [G_t^{(n)} - q(s_t, a_t)].$$
Implementation note: you can only perform this update *after* you have
observed $n$ more transitions, so updates lag by $n$ steps.
</details>

### Problem 4 — the bias-variance knob

State qualitatively how bias and variance of $G_t^{(n)}$ depend on $n$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Bias comes from bootstrapping $q$ (which is wrong); variance comes from
real random rewards.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Bias:** decreases with $n$ — more real rewards crowd out the biased
bootstrap. At $n = \infty$ (MC) bias = 0.
**Variance:** *increases* with $n$ — each extra reward injects its own
randomness. At $n = 1$ variance ≈ $\sigma_r^2$; at $n = \infty$ variance is
$O(\sigma_r^2 / (1-\gamma^2))$.
The optimal $n$ balances the two.
</details>

### Problem 5 — reward propagation

Imagine a chain of length $L$ with reward only at the end. With $n = 1$
Sarsa, how many episodes are needed for the reward signal to reach state 0?
With $n$-step Sarsa?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$n = 1$: each episode propagates the reward signal back **one state**, so
$L - 1$ episodes are needed. $n$-step: each episode propagates back **n
states** (the bootstrap target now sees the reward directly when within $n$
of the goal). So $\lceil (L - 1) / n \rceil$ episodes. **n-step is faster
on long chains.**
</details>

### Problem 6 — implementation memory

How many time-step tuples do you need to keep in a buffer to compute the
$n$-step update at time $t$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
You need the last $n + 1$ tuples: the state-action $(s_t, a_t)$ being
updated, plus $n$ subsequent rewards $r_{t+1}, \ldots, r_{t+n}$, plus the
next state-action $(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n})$. **Memory: $O(n)$**, much smaller than
storing whole episodes for MC.
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
$n$ is a knob that interpolates between TD's low-variance bias and MC's
zero-bias variance. The middle of the knob almost always beats the
endpoints — *empirically* on most benchmarks the sweet spot is $n \in [3, 10]$
depending on horizon length.
:::

::: {.fun-fact}
Sutton's 1988 TD($\lambda$) paper introduced an even cleverer construction:
exponentially weight all $n$-step returns by $(1 - \lambda) \lambda^{n-1}$.
This is the **λ-return**. $\lambda = 0$ recovers TD(0); $\lambda = 1$ recovers
MC; intermediate $\lambda$ has the same sweet spot $n$-step has, but
without picking a single $n$. The implementation uses *eligibility traces*
— a clever bookkeeping trick that avoids storing all the $n$-step returns
explicitly. We touch on this in the synthesis lesson but not in this track.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**$n$-step return:**
$$G_t^{(n)} \;=\; r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n} + \gamma^n \, q(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n}).$$

**$n$-step Sarsa update:**
$$q(s_t, a_t) \;\leftarrow\; q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \, [G_t^{(n)} - q(s_t, a_t)].$$

Special cases: $n = 1$ recovers **Sarsa**; $n = T - t$ (full episode)
recovers **Monte Carlo**.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Real rewards for n steps, then a bootstrap —*
> *Each step replaces a guess with a fact.*
> *Small n: low variance, high bias;*
> *Large n: low bias, high variance.*
> *Pick n to balance the two.*

## Apply

### Worked example — 3-step Sarsa update on a chain

> A chain $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to s_5 (\text{terminal})$ with
> rewards $r_2 = 0, r_3 = 0, r_4 = 0, r_5 = +1$. $\gamma = 0.9$,
> $\alpha = 0.5$. Initial $q$ are all $0$. Use $n = 3$ to update
> $q(s_1, \text{right})$ after observing the first 3 rewards.

A competent solver thinks like this:

1. **Buffer the trajectory:** $(s_1, \text{right})$, then rewards
   $r_2 = 0, r_3 = 0, r_4 = 0$, arriving at $(s_4, a_4)$ with
   $a_4 = \text{right}$.
2. **Compute $G_1^{(3)}$:** $0 + 0.9 \cdot 0 + 0.9^2 \cdot 0 + 0.9^3 \cdot q(s_4, a_4) = 0$.
   (Bootstrap is zero because $q$ is initialised to zero.)
3. **Update $q(s_1, \text{right})$:** $0 + 0.5 \cdot (0 - 0) = 0$. **No
   movement.** The reward at the goal isn't reached by the 3-step window
   from $s_1$.
4. Continuing: by the time we update $q(s_2, \text{right})$ (after rewards
   $r_3, r_4, r_5$), the 3-step return is $0 + 0 + 0.81 \cdot 1 = 0.81$,
   so $q(s_2, \text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.81 = 0.405$.

So 3-step Sarsa propagates the reward 3 states back per episode — versus
1 state for plain Sarsa. **On this 5-state chain, 3-step finishes in 1 episode
instead of 4.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $G_t^{(2)}$ given $r_{t+1} = 1$, $r_{t+2} = 2$,
$q(s_{t+2}, a_{t+2}) = 5$, $\gamma = 0.5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$G_t^{(2)} = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 + 0.25 \cdot 5 = 1 + 1 + 1.25 = 3.25$.
</details>

**W2.** Recover the Sarsa target by setting $n = 1$ in $G_t^{(n)}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$G_t^{(1)} = r_{t+1} + \gamma q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})$ — the Sarsa target.
</details>

**W3.** Show that as $n \to \infty$ (with episode length $T$), $G_t^{(n)} \to G_t$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For $n \ge T - t$, the bootstrap term $\gamma^n q(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n})$ becomes
$\gamma^{T-t} q(\text{terminal}) = 0$ by convention (terminal = 0). The
remaining sum is the full discounted return $G_t$. So **$n$-step recovers
MC at the largest $n$**.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A chain with rewards $r_2 = -1$, $r_3 = -1$, $r_4 = 10$ (terminal).
$\gamma = 0.9$. With $n = 2$, compute $G_2^{(2)}$ assuming $q(s_4, \cdot) = 0$
(terminal).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$G_2^{(2)} = r_3 + \gamma r_4 + \gamma^2 q(s_4, a_4) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot 10 + 0.81 \cdot 0 = 8$.
</details>

**P2.** *Bias-variance trade quantitatively.* Suppose every reward has
variance $\sigma^2 = 1$ and the current $q$ has error $|q(s, a) - q^\*(s, a)| \le \beta$ uniformly. Bound the variance and bias of $G_t^{(n)}$ in
terms of $n, \gamma, \sigma, \beta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Variance: sum of $n$ independent reward variances scaled by $\gamma^{2k}$
plus bootstrap variance. Bias: error of $q$ scaled by $\gamma^n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathrm{Var}(G_t^{(n)}) = \sigma^2 \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^{2k} + \gamma^{2n} \mathrm{Var}(q(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n})) \le \sigma^2 \frac{1 - \gamma^{2n}}{1 - \gamma^2} + \gamma^{2n} \beta^2$.
$|\text{Bias}| = |\gamma^n (\mathbb{E}[q] - q^\*)| \le \gamma^n \beta$.

So **bias decays exponentially in $n$**; **variance saturates at
$\sigma^2 / (1 - \gamma^2)$** for large $n$. The MSE-optimal $n$ is the
$n$ that balances the two — usually a small intermediate value when $\beta$
is moderate and $\sigma^2$ is small.
</details>

**P3.** Show $n$-step Sarsa is Dvoretzky-shaped per state-action pair, with
target $T_k = G_t^{(n)}$. Why is the *contracting target* condition
non-trivially needed?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The target depends on $q$ for $n$ steps in the future. The contraction has
to play nicely with the $n$-step Bellman operator.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The expected $n$-step Bellman operator $\mathcal{T}_\pi^n$ is a
$\gamma^n$-contraction in $\ell_\infty$, with the same fixed point $q_\pi$.
So Dvoretzky's contracting-target condition holds with contraction modulus
$\gamma^n$ — even *stronger* contraction per "useful step" than $n=1$, but
applied *less often* (only every $n$ time steps). The trade is a wash in
sample complexity but $n$-step is faster in *episode* count for long
chains.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** *Why does the optimal $n$ depend on the horizon?*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Reward propagation per episode versus variance per update.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Long horizon ⇒ more steps for reward to propagate ⇒ benefit of larger $n$
(fewer episodes to push reward back). But long horizon also accumulates
more reward variance ⇒ pressure for smaller $n$. The intersection of these
two scaling laws picks the optimum, usually $n \approx \sqrt{H}$ for
horizon $H$ on simple chains. Empirically Sutton-Barto's bench observes
$n \in [3, 10]$ for horizons up to ~100.
</details>

**S2.** Sketch how to derive **TD(λ)** from $n$-step returns.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Average all $G_t^{(n)}$ with weight $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The **λ-return** is $G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^\infty \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$. The weights sum to 1 (geometric series). $\lambda = 0$
collapses to $G_t^{(1)}$; $\lambda = 1$ collapses to $G_t^{(\infty)} = G_t$.
TD(λ) updates with this target. The naive implementation requires
unbounded memory — but **eligibility traces** compute it incrementally in
$O(|\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{A}|)$ memory per step. We don't expand TD(λ)
in this track; see Sutton-Barto Chapter 12.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$n$-step is biased because it bootstraps."** The bias *exists* but
   shrinks like $\gamma^n$ in $n$. For $\gamma = 0.99$ and $n = 10$, the
   bias is 90% smaller than $n=1$.
2. **"Larger $n$ is always better."** No — variance grows. The true winner
   is intermediate.
3. **"$n$-step needs to wait for the episode to end."** Wrong — only for
   $n$ steps. That's the point: it works in continuing tasks too.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else:

1. **$n$-step return:** $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} + \gamma^n q(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n})$.
2. **n is the bias-variance knob.** Small $n$ = low variance, biased; large
   $n$ = high variance, unbiased; intermediate often best.
3. **Reward propagates $n$ states per episode.** Larger $n$ means faster
   credit assignment on long chains.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you write down $G_t^{(n)}$ from memory and recover Sarsa and MC as
   special cases?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 8 practice problems without peeking past
   *Hint 1*?
3. Can you state, in one breath, why intermediate $n$ usually beats both
   endpoints?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Lesson 26 — Q-Learning](../26-q-learning/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Working P2 — the bias-variance bound is the punchline
  of the lesson.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problems 4 and 5. The knob and the
  reward-propagation argument together explain everything.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[MFRL — 24, Sarsa](../24-sarsa/index.qmd)** — the $n=1$ case.
- **[MFRL — 17, MC Basic](../17-mc-basic/index.qmd)** — the $n=\infty$
  case.
- **[MFRL — 23, TD(0)](../23-td-state-values/index.qmd)** — the bias-
  variance framing.

### What this unlocks

- **[MFRL — 26, Q-Learning](../26-q-learning/index.qmd)** — same $n=1$
  framework, off-policy.
- **[MFRL — 27, TD Methods Unified](../27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)** —
  $n$-step appears as one axis of the design space.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Modern policy-gradient methods (A3C, PPO, IMPALA) use
$n$-step returns as their value-function targets — typically $n \in [5, 20]$
— because plain $n=1$ is too biased for deep-network estimators. The
*Generalised Advantage Estimator* (GAE) is the $\lambda$-return version of
the $n$-step idea, applied to advantage functions.
See [Bellman equation](/glossary.qmd#bellman-equation) and
[learning rate](/glossary.qmd#learning-rate).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this track
expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Source:** Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement
  Learning* (Springer 2025), Chapter 7 §7.3. Cited freely; never transcribed.
- **Touchstones honoured:** Russian school problem-first sequencing,
  Sutton-Barto bias-variance framing, Bhāskara verse for the n-knob.
- **Self-study guarantees:** pre-flight ✓, multiple representations
  (widget, recursion, English, chain) ✓, graduated hints ✓, worked
  solutions ✓, common-confusions ✓, self-assessment ✓.
:::
